title: Deduction for Life Insurance Premiums and Specified Investments
description: Section 80C of the Income Tax Act 1961 allows deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh per financial year for specified investments and payments, including life insurance premiums. Available to individuals and HUFs. Deduction is from Gross Total Income (GTI).
conditions: Only for individual and HUF taxpayers
For life insurance premiums: policy must be in own name, spouse's name, or children's name (any dependent child, not just minor children)
Premium must not exceed 10% of sum assured for policies issued on or after 01/04/2012 (for older policies: 20%)
If premium exceeds 10% of sum assured: only 10% of sum assured is deductible (proportionate restriction)
Premium must be for qualifying life insurance (not premium for motor, health, or general insurance)
Section 80D handles health insurance premiums separately
Available only in Old Tax Regime (NOT available in New Tax Regime under Section 115BAC)

limits.life_insurance_sub_limit: No separate sub-limit within ₹1.5 lakh — competes with PPF, ELSS, NPS, NSC, home loan principal, school fees, etc.

included_investments: Life insurance premiums (LIC or private insurer)
ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme) — mutual funds
examples: Annual LIC premium ₹40,000 (sum assured ₹6 lakh = 15× premium, qualifies). Add PPF ₹80,000. Add ELSS ₹30,000. Total ₹1,50,000 = full ₹1.5 lakh deduction.
LIC premium ₹2 lakh, sum assured ₹10 lakh (premium = 20% of SA, exceeds 10% limit). Eligible deduction = 10% × ₹10 lakh = ₹1 lakh only. Balance ₹1 lakh from premium is not deductible.
Tax saving at 30% bracket: ₹1.5 lakh deduction × 30% × 1.04 (surcharge) = ₹46,800 saved.
notes: This deduction is available ONLY in Old Tax Regime. Under New Tax Regime (default from AY 2024-25), no 80C deductions.
Amount paid as premium in excess of 10% of sum assured (or 20% for pre-2012 policies) is NEITHER deductible under 80C NOR tax-free at maturity under 10(10D).
ULIP premiums qualify under 80C. But if ULIP annual premium > ₹2.5 lakh, maturity is taxable even if within 80C limit.
Single premium life insurance: 20% of sum assured OR actual premium, whichever is less, is deductible. Subject to the ₹1.5 lakh aggregate limit.
Premium for keyman insurance (employer paying on employee's life) is NOT eligible for 80C deduction.
documents_for_tax_filing: Form 26AS to verify TDS if applicable

title: Deduction for Health Insurance Premiums
description: Section 80D allows deduction for premiums paid for health insurance policies for self, spouse, dependent children, and parents. Available to individuals and HUFs. This is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1.5 lakh 80C limit.
conditions: Premium must be for health insurance or critical illness rider
Payment must be by any mode EXCEPT cash (cheque, credit card, UPI, NEFT accepted)
Preventive health checkup (up to ₹5,000 within the main limit): cash payment allowed
Policy must be from IRDAI-registered insurer
Deduction available under both Old Tax Regime and partially under New Tax Regime (only for senior citizen parents)
Group health insurance premium paid by employer is NOT eligible for 80D by employee

limits.self_spouse_children_senior_citizen: ₹50,000 per year (if any of them is a senior citizen)
limits.maximum_deduction_all_senior: ₹1,00,000 (if both self and parents are senior citizens)
limits.maximum_deduction_mixed: ₹75,000 (if only parents are senior, self is not)
limits.preventive_health_checkup: ₹5,000 within the respective limit (can be for self or family member)

examples: Age 35. Health premium self+spouse+kids = ₹18,000. Parents (60+) premium = ₹30,000. Total deduction = ₹18,000 + ₹30,000 = ₹48,000. (₹18,000 < ₹25,000 limit; ₹30,000 < ₹50,000 limit).
Age 50 (senior citizen). Own health premium = ₹35,000. Parents (75+) premium = ₹45,000. Deduction = ₹35,000 + ₹45,000 = ₹80,000. (₹35,000 < ₹50,000 senior limit; ₹45,000 < ₹50,000 senior limit).
Maximum deduction: both you (62) and parents (85+) are senior citizens. Your premium ₹50,000 + parents ₹50,000 = ₹1,00,000 deduction. Tax saving at 30% = ₹31,200.
notes: GST on health insurance premium is also included in the deductible amount.
80D deduction for senior citizen parents is available under New Tax Regime (Section 115BAC) also. This is an exception to the general rule.
Multi-year health insurance: deduction available only for the proportionate premium for the year being assessed.
Critical illness riders and disease-specific health plans also qualify under 80D.
Contribution to Central Government Health Scheme (CGHS) qualifies under 80D for government employees.
documents_for_tax_filing: Premium paid certificate from insurer (if annual receipt not available)
Form 16/salary slip showing health insurance deduction (if employer pays)

title: Exemption for Life Insurance Maturity and Death Benefits
description: Section 10(10D) exempts life insurance maturity proceeds and death benefits from income tax. Death benefit is ALWAYS fully exempt. Maturity proceeds have conditions based on policy type and premium amount.
conditions: DEATH BENEFIT: Always fully exempt under 10(10D) — no conditions, no limits
MATURITY BENEFIT (standard policies): Exempt IF annual premium does not exceed 10% of sum assured
MATURITY BENEFIT (policies issued before 01/04/2012): Exempt if premium ≤ 20% of sum assured
MATURITY BENEFIT (ULIPs issued after 01/02/2021): Exempt ONLY IF annual premium ≤ ₹2,50,000
MATURITY BENEFIT (ULIPs with premium > ₹2.5 lakh/year): Subject to 10% LTCG tax (like equity mutual funds). ₹1 lakh exemption applies.
MATURITY BENEFIT (traditional endowment/money back issued after 01/04/2023): Exempt only if premium ≤ ₹5,00,000/year. Above ₹5 lakh: fully taxable as income.
If premium condition fails: entire maturity amount (not just gains) is added to income and taxed at applicable slab rate

limits.standard_premium_to_sa_ratio: Premium must be ≤ 10% of sum assured
limits.ltcg_exemption: ₹1,00,000 per year on equity ULIP gains (same as equity mutual fund)

examples: Term plan ₹1 crore, annual premium ₹12,000. Death benefit = ₹1 crore. Fully exempt. (Premium/SA = 1.2%, well below 10%)
Endowment plan: annual premium ₹50,000, SA ₹5 lakh (premium = 10% of SA = borderline case). Maturity proceeds = ₹18 lakh. Fully exempt under 10(10D).
ULIP: annual premium ₹3 lakh (exceeds ₹2.5 lakh threshold). Fund value at maturity ₹50 lakh, cost ₹42 lakh, LTCG = ₹8 lakh. Tax = 10% × (₹8 lakh - ₹1 lakh exemption) = 10% × ₹7 lakh = ₹70,000.
High-premium endowment (FY 2023-24+): annual premium ₹6 lakh, SA ₹60 lakh. Premium > ₹5 lakh limit. Maturity ₹1.2 crore: ENTIRE ₹1.2 crore is added to income (not just gains).
notes: Budget 2021 change: ULIPs with premium > ₹2.5 lakh/year are taxed like equity mutual funds from maturity year.
Budget 2023 change: Traditional policies (endowment, money back) with premium > ₹5 lakh/year are fully taxable at maturity.
If multiple policies: aggregate annual premium of all non-ULIP policies matters. If total > ₹5 lakh, all above ₹5 lakh threshold is taxable.
Death benefit remains 100% tax-free in ALL cases — including ULIPs with high premiums.
These changes DO NOT affect existing policies bought before the respective budget dates.
TDS implications: insurers deduct 5% TDS on maturity if PAN provided, 20% if PAN not provided, for taxable policies.
documents_for_tax_filing: Policy document showing sum assured and premium amount
Form 16A if TDS deducted by insurer

title: Goods and Services Tax on Insurance Premiums
description: GST is levied on insurance premiums as these constitute supply of services. Rate varies by insurance type. GST is charged over and above the base premium.
conditions: GST is included in the invoice/receipt issued by the insurer
For 80C and 80D deduction purposes, premium including GST is eligible for deduction
Life insurance (term, endowment, money back): different GST rates apply
Health insurance: uniform 18% GST
No input tax credit available to individual policyholders

limits.ulip_charges: 18% GST on insurance-related charges (mortality, policy admin charges); fund management charge is exempt

examples: Health insurance premium ₹10,000. GST 18% = ₹1,800. Total payable = ₹11,800. Eligible for 80D deduction: ₹11,800 (including GST).
Term insurance premium ₹12,000. GST 18% = ₹2,160. Total = ₹14,160. Eligible for 80C deduction: ₹14,160 (including GST, subject to 80C aggregate limit).
LIC endowment first year: base premium ₹50,000, GST 4.5% = ₹2,250. Total ₹52,250. Renewal year: GST 2.25% = ₹1,125, total ₹51,125.
notes: The industry and consumer groups have lobbied for reducing health insurance GST from 18% to 0% (exempt). This remains an ongoing policy debate.
GST Council has not yet granted exemption to health insurance as of 2025.
For group health insurance bought by employer: company can claim input tax credit on GST paid.
Bima Sugam transactions may eventually get GST simplification.
documents_for_tax_filing: Premium receipts showing GST amount separately
GSTIN of insurer (for business policyholders claiming ITC)

title: ULIP Tax Treatment — Complete Guide
description: ULIPs have complex tax treatment across purchase, holding period, and maturity/redemption. Tax rules changed significantly in Budget 2021. This guide covers the complete ULIP tax lifecycle.
conditions: PREMIUM (entry): Eligible for 80C deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh aggregate
FUND SWITCHES: Switching between funds within ULIP is NOT a taxable event (unlike MF switching)
PARTIAL WITHDRAWAL (after lock-in): Exempt from tax if policy qualifies under 10(10D)
MATURITY (pre-Feb 2021 policies): Fully exempt under 10(10D) regardless of premium amount
MATURITY (policies from Feb 2021+): Exempt only if annual premium ≤ ₹2.5 lakh
MATURITY (policies from Feb 2021+, premium > ₹2.5 lakh): Taxed as LTCG at 10% with ₹1 lakh exemption
DEATH BENEFIT: Always fully exempt under 10(10D)
SURRENDER (after 5 years lock-in): Same tax treatment as maturity

limits.lock_in_period: 5 years (mandatory) — partial withdrawal not allowed before 5 years
limits.premium_exemption_threshold: ₹2,50,000 annual premium for policies from Feb 2021

examples: ULIP bought 2022: annual premium ₹1.5 lakh. After 15 years, fund value ₹45 lakh. 10(10D) fully exempt because premium ≤ ₹2.5 lakh. Zero tax on ₹45 lakh maturity.

ULIP bought 2022: annual premium ₹3 lakh. After 10 years, fund value ₹50 lakh. Premium > ₹2.5 lakh, taxable. Gains = ₹50L - (₹3L × 10 years) = ₹20 lakh. Tax = 10% × (₹20L - ₹1L exemption) = 10% × ₹19L = ₹1.9 lakh.
Multiple ULIPs: ULIP A premium ₹2L, ULIP B premium ₹1L. Total = ₹3L > ₹2.5L threshold. Both ULIPs become taxable at maturity.
notes: The ₹2.5 lakh threshold applies to AGGREGATE premium across all ULIPs bought from Feb 2021.
Pre-Feb 2021 ULIPs are grandfathered — they remain fully exempt under old rules.

ULIP vs Mutual Fund: ULIP has higher charges (mortality) but fund switches are not taxable, while MF switches are taxable. For very long term (20+ years) with moderate switching, ULIPs with low premium (<₹2.5L) can be competitive.
ULIP partial withdrawals after 5 years: allowed (minimum 2 times per year usually). Tax-free if policy qualifies.

title: NPS Contribution Deduction — Comparison with Insurance
description: Section 80CCD allows additional deduction for NPS (National Pension System) contributions. Often compared with insurance for tax planning. NPS + Insurance together can maximize tax deductions.
conditions: 80CCD(1): Employee/voluntary contribution to NPS — within the ₹1.5 lakh 80C aggregate limit
80CCD(1B): Additional ₹50,000 deduction for own NPS contribution — OVER AND ABOVE ₹1.5 lakh 80C limit
80CCD(2): Employer NPS contribution — exempt up to 14% of basic salary for government, 10% for private sector employees
NPS Tier 1 is eligible; Tier 2 is NOT eligible for deduction
On maturity (60 years): 60% of corpus is tax-free; 40% must be used to buy annuity (taxable income)

limits.within_80c_limit: ₹1,50,000 (shared with life insurance, PPF, ELSS, etc.)

examples: Maximum tax saving with insurance + NPS: 80C (LIC ₹50K + PPF ₹1L = ₹1.5L) + 80D (health ₹25K) + 80CCD(1B) NPS ₹50K = ₹2.25 lakh total deduction. At 30% bracket = ₹70,200 tax saving.
Employer NPS ₹60,000 (10% of ₹6L basic salary) under 80CCD(2): fully exempt from income tax, no limit applicable.
notes: NPS is better than insurance for pure retirement savings: lower costs, market returns, more transparent.
Insurance + NPS combo: use term insurance for protection (80C), NPS for retirement (80CCD), health insurance for medical (80D).
NPS annuity at retirement is fully taxable — plan this in your retirement tax strategy.
For complete tax optimization: term plan (minimal premium but qualifies for 80C) + maxed 80D (health insurance) + NPS ₹50K (80CCD 1B).
documents_for_tax_filing: NPS contribution receipt from CRA (NSDL or CAMS)

title: TDS on Insurance Payouts
description: Tax Deducted at Source applicable on certain insurance payouts. Insurers are required to deduct TDS before disbursing proceeds in specified cases.
conditions: Section 194DA: TDS on life insurance maturity proceeds if NOT exempt under 10(10D)
TDS rate with PAN: 5% on the income (gain = proceeds minus premiums paid)
TDS rate without PAN: 20% on full payment
Health insurance reimbursements: No TDS
Death benefit: No TDS (fully exempt under 10(10D))
Survival benefits (money back): No TDS if within 10(10D) exemption
Taxable ULIP maturity (premium > ₹2.5L): TDS at 5% on gain portion

limits.tds_threshold: If net payment (gross payout minus premiums paid) does not exceed ₹1 lakh, no TDS applicable

examples: Taxable endowment maturity: gross ₹30 lakh, premiums paid ₹18 lakh, gain = ₹12 lakh. TDS = 5% × ₹12 lakh = ₹60,000 deducted by insurer. File ITR to account for this TDS.
Small policy maturity: gross ₹2.5 lakh, premiums paid ₹2 lakh, net = ₹50,000 (< ₹1 lakh threshold). No TDS applicable.
notes: Always provide PAN to your insurer — prevents 20% TDS.
TDS deducted appears in Form 26AS. Claim credit in your ITR.
If TDS deducted exceeds your actual tax liability, you can claim refund in ITR.
Insurer issues Form 16A for TDS deducted on insurance payouts.
documents_for_tax_filing: Policy document and premium payment receipts to calculate actual gains

title: New Tax Regime Impact on Insurance Tax Benefits
description: Budget 2020 introduced the New Tax Regime (lower slab rates, no deductions). Default from FY 2024-25. Impact on insurance deductions is significant — most insurance-related deductions are NOT available under the New Tax Regime.
conditions: New Tax Regime: Lower slab rates (5% → 25% max) but MOST deductions disallowed
Deductions NOT available under New Tax Regime: 80C (insurance premium, PPF, ELSS), 80D (most health insurance)
Deductions STILL AVAILABLE under New Tax Regime: 80CCD(2) (employer NPS), 80D for senior citizen parents (limited)
Tax exemption 10(10D) for insurance maturity: Still applies under New Tax Regime
Section 80CCD(1B) own NPS contribution: NOT available under New Tax Regime from FY 2023-24
Standard deduction ₹75,000 available under New Tax Regime from FY 2024-25

limits.80D_new_regime: Only for senior citizen parents (partial availability)
limits.10_10D_exemption: STILL available in New Tax Regime

examples: Old Regime: Income ₹15L, save ₹46,800 via 80C + ₹15,600 via 80D = ₹62,400 total. Net tax on ₹15L = ₹1,87,100.
New Regime: Income ₹15L, no 80C/80D deductions. But slab rates lower. Net tax on ₹15L = ₹1,50,000. New regime better for this income level if deductions are fewer.
Decision tip: If your 80C + 80D + other deductions > ₹3-4 lakh, Old Regime is usually better. Below that threshold, New Regime may save more.
notes: The 10(10D) exemption (life insurance maturity) applies regardless of which tax regime you choose.
Buying life insurance or health insurance is still a GOOD DECISION even under New Tax Regime — for protection value, not tax benefit.
Under New Regime, health insurance and term insurance still provide essential financial protection even without the 80D/80C benefit.
If you switch from Old to New Regime: accumulated 80C investments (PPF, ULIP) don't need to be liquidated — just new deductions stop.
documents_for_tax_filing: Form 10-IE if opting for New Tax Regime (for business income)
Declaration to employer for TDS calculation under chosen regime
